description: Length-matched training challenge facts for LLMs.

facts.context: Neural scaling laws predict performance improvement with:
facts.truth: power-law relationships between loss and compute, data, and parameters
distractors: linear proportional improvement as each resource is scaled up uniformly
sudden discontinuous jumps occurring at specific critical model size thresholds
no predictable or consistent relationship between resources and final loss

facts.context: Emergent abilities in LLMs refer to:
facts.truth: capabilities appearing at certain scales, not predicted by smaller models
distractors: gradual linear improvement that is smooth and predictable across all scales
abilities that are uniformly present at every model size from the smallest
a debunked concept shown to be entirely a measurement artifact of metrics

facts.context: Chinchilla scaling laws suggest that many LLMs were:
facts.truth: undertrained, needing more tokens relative to parameters for optimal compute
distractors: overtrained with excessive data relative to the number of model parameters
trained at the optimal compute-efficient ratio already by standard practice
limited primarily by parameter count rather than by training data quantity

facts.context: Training data quality affects LLMs by:
facts.truth: directly influencing model capabilities, biases, and factual accuracy
distractors: having negligible effect when the total quantity of tokens is sufficient
only affecting rare edge cases while core capabilities remain unaffected
being fully correctable through post-training alignment techniques alone

facts.context: Training data deduplication is important because:
facts.truth: duplicates cause memorization and degrade generalization performance
distractors: repeated examples improve learning by reinforcing important data patterns
removing duplicates consistently hurts final model performance on all tasks
duplicate frequency has no measurable effect on model behavior or outputs

facts.truth: test data appears in training, inflating reported performance scores
distractors: contamination between training and test sets is impossible to occur in practice
seeing test examples during training genuinely improves real model capabilities
all modern benchmark suites fully prevent any form of training contamination

facts.context: Catastrophic forgetting during fine-tuning refers to:
facts.truth: loss of pretrained capabilities when adapting to new downstream tasks
distractors: perfect retention of all prior knowledge throughout the fine-tuning process
a phenomenon affecting only small models with insufficient parameter capacity
a completely solved problem using current modern fine-tuning methodology

facts.context: Training instability in large LLMs manifests as:
facts.truth: loss spikes requiring intervention, with causes not fully understood
distractors: consistently smooth training loss curves at every model scale and setting
instability occurring only in small models while large models train stably
fully predictable and preventable behavior using standard hyperparameters

facts.truth: requires careful adaptation; settings don't all transfer from small to large
distractors: works perfectly with identical hyperparameters applying at every model scale
larger models require the exact same settings as their smaller counterparts
small-scale hyperparameter tuning is fully sufficient for all larger models

facts.context: Training data mixture (proportions of different sources) affects:
facts.truth: model capabilities in domain-dependent ways that vary with composition
distractors: nothing at all if the total number of training tokens is held at a fixed amount
only training throughput speed and not final capabilities or model performance
nothing at sufficient scale since data mixture becomes irrelevant for large models

facts.truth: training small additional parameters while freezing most pretrained weights
distractors: permanently reducing the total model size through structured weight pruning
training all model parameters simultaneously but with improved efficiency
functioning only for text classification and not for open-ended generation

facts.context: Curriculum learning for LLMs (ordering training data) has shown:
facts.truth: mixed results, with benefits depending on task and implementation details
distractors: consistent large improvements across all model sizes and task configurations
uniformly harmful effects on model performance regardless of implementation
that training data ordering never matters for any large language model training
